Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Cross Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-24-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN FORD, AND CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Leave To File Cross Complaint.

Defendants City and County of San Francisco and John Ford's motion for leave to file a cross-complaint is granted. Plaintiff does not dispute that the Code of Civil Procedure allows the cross-complaint, arguing only that the City "waited 10 whole months." (Opp. 3:16-17.) But the City explains it was trying to settle the dispute (Rply. 2:17-26), and the trial date is not until June 9, 2025, so any prejudice will be minor.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-20-2023 Continued To Mar-06-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

City And County Of San Francisco And John George Ford'S Notice Of Settlement And Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Of Settlement
Off Calendar Nov-20-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT JOHN FORD, CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO City And County Of San Francisco And John George Ford'S Notice Of Settlement And Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Of Settlement

Defendants City and County of San Francisco and John George Ford's motion for order determining the good faith of settlement is denied without prejudice. Assuming arguendo that CCP 877.6 applies to these circumstances, there is not substantial evidence showing that the settlement is in the "ballpark."

When determining whether a settlement was entered into in good faith the court should take into account a rough approximation of plaintiff's total recovery and defendants' proportionate liability, the amount paid in settlement, and recognize that a settlor should pay less in settlement than if he were found liable after trial. The court should also consider the financial condition of the settlor, the existence of insurance, and any evidence of collusion. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assoc. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.)

"The party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue." (CCP 877.6(d).) However, once Amguard Insurance Company attacked the settlement as lacking good faith, settlors were required to file counteraffidavits to make an evidentiary showing that the settlement was "in the ballpark." Settlors must provide substantial evidence to support a critical assumption as to the nature and extent of its liability. (Mattco Forge, Inc. v. Arthur Young & Co. (1995) 38 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1350, n.6.)

Here, settlors rely on counsel Kwan's moving and reply declarations. Those declarations fail to provide any information regarding plaintiff Grajeda's damages and a rough approximation of what he could recover. Given that evidentiary gap, the court has no idea whether the $20,000 settlement is in good faith.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer: W. Trupek, Not reported (302/RBU)
